coredata.dc:title: Ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal synthesis of ZnIn2S4/graphene-quantum-dots nanowires and the sono-effect improved catalytic activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yujie
Sun, Lingling
Li, Xiaoli
Sun, Xianke
coredata.dc:description: Composite ZnIn2S4/graphene-quantum-dots (ZnIn2S4/GQDs) nanowires with excellent catalytic activities were synthesized using an ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal route. The catalytic activities of ZnIn2S4/GQDs nanowires with different GQDs contents were evaluated by degrading tetracycline hydrochloride. The incorporation of GQDs not only leads to higher specific surface area and thus results in more reactive sites but also enhances light absorbance. Meanwhile, the photocurrent response and EIS curves show that the incorporation of GQDs results in higher efficient separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. These bring about the enhancement of degradation efficiency. With the help of ultrasonic, the degradation efficiency is further enhanced due to the synergistic sonochemical and photochemical effects. The reactive radical of •OH plays dominant role in degradation process. The possible degradation pathways were speculated using high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnIn2S4/GQDs
Nanowires
Ultrasonic
Sono-photocatalysis
Synergistic effects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (A) and Raman spectra (B) of the obtained catalysts.
SEM (A) and TEM (B) images of ZnIn2S4; SEM (C), TEM (D) and elemental mapping (E-H) images of ZnIn2S4/GQD5.
Specific XPS spectra of Zn 2p (A), In 3d (B), S 2p (C) and C 1s (D) for ZnIn2S4/GQD5.
UV–vis absorbance spectra and Tauc plots of the synthesized catalysts.
Photocurrent density (A) and EIS spectra (B) of the synthesized catalysts.
Degradation efficiencies of tetracycline hydrochloride in sonocatalysis (A), photocatalysis (B) and sono-photocatalysis (C).

Curves of Ln
C 0
/
C t
vs. time after adding synthesized catalysts in sonocatalysis (A), photocatalysis (B) and sono-photocatalysis (C).
ESR curves of DMPO-•OH without catalyst and with the presence of GQDs, ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD4 in sonocatalysis (A), photocatalysis (B) and sono-photocatalysis (C).
Band structures of the synthesized catalysts and proposed photocatalytic mechanism.
Proposed photodegradation pathways of tetracycline hydrochloride.

head.ce:title: Ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal synthesis of ZnIn2S4/graphene-quantum-dots nanowires and the sono-effect improved catalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Composite ZnIn2S4/graphene-quantum-dots (ZnIn2S4/GQDs) nanowires with excellent catalytic activities were synthesized using an ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal route. The catalytic activities of ZnIn2S4/GQDs nanowires with different GQDs contents were evaluated by degrading tetracycline hydrochloride. The incorporation of GQDs not only leads to higher specific surface area and thus results in more reactive sites but also enhances light absorbance. Meanwhile, the photocurrent response and EIS curves show that the incorporation of GQDs results in higher efficient separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. These bring about the enhancement of degradation efficiency. With the help of ultrasonic, the degradation efficiency is further enhanced due to the synergistic sonochemical and photochemical effects. The reactive radical of •OH plays dominant role in degradation process. The possible degradation pathways were speculated using high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is one of conspicuous ‘green’ technologies in both environmental remediation and energy conversion because that it can degrade pollutants and generate clean energies by harvesting solar energy [1]. But as a whole, photocatalysts must be good absorber of solar light and semiconductors to produce charge carriers with the potential to promote redox reactions. And for most of photocatalysts, the drawbacks limiting their practical supplications are their low absorbance efficiencies to solar energy, limited mass transfer of pollutants to the photocatalyst surface, and uncontrolled recombination of charge carriers [2,3]. In order to enhance solar light absorbance-efficiency and charge carriers' separation-efficiency, surface modifications by carbon materials, such as graphene [4,5], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [6,7], carbon nanotubes/nanofibers/quantum dots [8–10], receive a great attention. Meanwhile, ultrasonic wastewater treatment technique is concerned because of its degradation ability to organic matter without the presence of chemicals, which avoids possible pollution coming from the chemicals [3]. However, this technology also has deficiencies: on one hand, the post-treatment must be carried out because that ultrasonic process can only break down organic contaminant into smaller molecules, and the produced molecules need further treatments to avoid secondary pollution; On the other hand, it causes high energy consumption since the equipment of ultrasonic treatment will consume a large amount of energy [11]. In order to improve degradation efficiency of pollutants and decrease energy consumption, sonocatalysis and sono-photocatalysis are developed. In these processes, the degradation efficiency is enhanced effectively on account of synergistic sonochemical and photochemical effects [12,13]. For example, A. Serra et al. stated that the sono-photocatalytic mineralization of antibiotics synergizing visible LED light and weak ultrasonic irradiation in the presence of Ca-doped ZnO nanopowders presents an outstanding start to develop highly efficient, long-term, eco-friendly catalysts for efficiently treating tetracycline [14]. And some other reports also proved that some semiconductors or composites can obtain outstanding catalytic activities in sonocatalysis and sono-photocatalysis, such as CaCu3Ti4O12 [15], FeVO4/BiVO4 [16], Ag6Si2O7/SmFeO3 [17], hollow CdS@HD-CoNi-LDH [18].

Graphene has a two-dimensional single planar sheet structure, which consists of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms linked by strong covalent bonds [19]. In recent years, lots of graphene-incorporated semiconductor photocatalysts have been fabricated in order to heighten light absorbance, pollutant adsorption and separation of charge carriers [20]. graphene-quantum-dots (GQDs) is a class of graphene derivative and receives a great attention on account of its good biocompatibility and photostability, plenty of raw materials, and high specific surface area [21]. For photocatalysts with the incorporation of GQDs, the incorporated GQDs introduce enhancive visible light response and lead to more efficient generation of charge carriers, act as the electron acceptors and result in higher efficient separation of charge carriers, and enhance contaminant adsorption. For example, Abulizi et al. reported that the introduction of GQDs in the g-C3N4/BiOCl Z-type system suppress the recombination of electron-hole (
e −
−
h +
) pairs due to rapid transfer of photoelectrons from conduction band to GQDs and thus leads to a generation rate of NH3 as much as 7.3 and 5.2 times of pure g-C3N4 and BiOCl [22]. To date, some of other GQDs incorporated photocatalysts also show higher activities in various catalysis processes, such as ZnO/GQDs [23], Bi2WO6/GQDs [24], TiO2/GQDs [25], and carbon nitride/GQDs [26].

Zinc indium sulfide (ZnIn2S4) belongs to the ternary metal chalcogenide semiconductor family and has a tunable band gap from 2.06 to 2.85 eV [27]. The relatively narrow band indicates that it is a visible responsive semiconductor. In addition, comparing with other metal sulfides, ZnIn2S4 has the merits of higher physicochemical stability, less toxic, and simpler preparation process [27,28]. As a result, ZnIn2S4 is considered as a great potential in photocatalysis. However, bare ZnIn2S4 generally shows inefficient photocatalytic ability on account of the relatively few active sites, highly efficient charge carrier recombination and relatively low photoconversion efficiency [29]. Following the ever-increasing attention on ZnIn2S4, a good deal of designing and modification routes have been carried out to propel practical photocatalysis applications, such as to increase light absorbance efficiency, active sites and charge transport efficiency via morphology/structure control [30,31] and heterojunction [32,33], to accelerate the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers via metal [34] or non-metal doping [35], to activate the molecular oxygen efficiently and thus enhance reactive oxygen species via atomic cluster [36].

In photocatalysis processes, it is well accepted that the reaction generally takes place between the surface of photocatalysts and pollutants. That is to say, the arrangement and coordination of surface atoms, as well as mass transfer of pollutants affect the photocatalytic performance. Generally, the surface performance can be modified via controlling the microstructure of the nano-structured photocatalyst. For example, one dimensional nanowire generally shows a merit of relatively short radial transmission distance, which helps to separate photoinduced charge carriers [37]. Meanwhile, ultrasonic leads to better dispersion of photocatalysts, ensures enough reaction sites for degradation, and improves mass transfer of contaminant towards photocatalysts [38]. Thus, in this work, we prepared ZnIn2S4 and GQDs incorporated ZnIn2S4 (ZnIn2S4/GQDs) nanowires using an ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal route. Their physical and chemical performance was characterized. And their catalytic performance in different catalysis processes was investigated by degrading tetracycline hydrochloride. The results show that incorporated GQDs enhance the catalytic activity of pure ZnIn2S4 and the synergistic sonochemical and photochemical effects further enhance catalytic activity.

GQDs were prepared using an ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal route. In a typical preparation, 10 mg of maltose (C12H22O11•H2O, AR, Aladdin) was dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water under magnetic stirring for 20 min. Next, 30 mL of hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36%w/w, Aladdin) was poured into the above solution under magnetic stirring for 15 min. Then, the mixture was heated in a microwave chemical reactor (640 W) for 15 min. Finally, the product was separated from the solution by centrifuging, washed with ultrapure water for 5 times and dried at 60 °C in vacuum.

ZnIn2S4 nanowires were prepared via an ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal route by using polycarbonate (PC, Aladdin) membranes with pore sizes of 0.1 μm in diameter as the template. In a typical synthesis, 0.885 g of indium chloride hydrate (InCl3, 99.99 %, Aladdin), 0.272 g of zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 99.95 %, Aladdin) and 0.601 g of thioacetamide (C2H5NS, ≥99.0 %, Aladdin) were dissolved into 50 mL of ultrapure water under magnetic stirring for 20 min. Next, the pH value of the solution was adjusted to be 2.5 by adding appropriate HCl solution (36%w/w, Aladdin). Then, a PC membrane was immersed in the above solution. And the ultrasonic processing was carried out in order to remove air in the pores of PC membrane and promote the precursor solution to fill the pores. Subsequently, the mixture was placed to a Schlenk line for the purpose of further removing air in the pores of PC membrane and promoting the precursor solution to fill the pores. The processes of ultrasonic processing and evacuation of air were cycled for 5 times. After that, the mixture was heated in a microwave chemical reactor (640 W) for 8 h. The products were collected, washed with ultrapure water for 5 times, and immersed in dichloromethane (CH2Cl2, 99.5 %, Aladdin) for 10 min to remove PC membrane. Finally, it was washed respectively with ultrapure water and ethanol for 5 times and dried at 60 °C in vacuum.

For the preparation of ZnIn2S4/GQDs, the process was similar with that of ZnIn2S4. In the mixed solution of InCl3, ZnCl2 and C2H5NS, appropriate GQDs were added. The weights of GODs were 10 mg, 15 mg, 20 mg, 25 mg and 30 mg, which were labeled as ZnIn2S4/GQD1, ZnIn2S4/GQD2, ZnIn2S4/GQD3, ZnIn2S4/GQD4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD5, respectively. The suspension was heated in a microwave chemical reactor (640 W) for 8 h. The subsequent process was same with that of pure ZnIn2S4.

The measurements of X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis absorbance spectra and photochemistry were carried out to investigate their physical and chemical performance. The catalytic activity was evaluated by degrading tetracycline hydrochloride and the intermediates in catalysis processes were investigated. These details were provided in Supporting Materials.

The phase and composition of the prepared catalysts are represented via XRD and Raman data. Fig. 1A provides XRD patterns of a series of prepared catalysts. The diffraction peaks for all the catalysts conform to the normative XRD data of hexagonal ZnIn2S4 (65–2023), indicating the pure phase of the prepared catalysts. And there are no diffraction peaks corresponding to GQDs, which may be induced by the low concentration of GQDs in the composites. The intensities of diffraction peaks for the GQDs incorporated ZnIn2S4 catalysts are higher than that of pure ZnIn2S4, meaning that GQDs may promote the crystallization of ZnIn2S4 to a certain extent. But the reason for it needs further investigation. To confirm GQDs in the composites, Raman measurements, as an alternative approach, were carried out. Fig. 1B provides the Raman spectra of ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD5. The bands peaking at about 245, 300, 344 and 376 cm−1 are assigned to the longitudinal (LO1), transverse (TO2), longitudinal (LO2) optical modes and A1g mode of ZnIn2S4, respectively [39]. And for the ZnIn2S4/GQD5 catalyst, two additional bands peaking at about 1330 and 1583 cm−1 appear. These two bands root in the D and G bands of GQDs, which are induced by symmetry breaking at defects and edges, and in-plane CC deformations, respectively [40]. The XRD and Raman results preliminarily demonstrate the successful preparation of ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs catalysts.

The morphology and composition of the prepared catalysts were represented by the electron microscope and elemental mapping photos. Fig. 2A and B provide the SEM/TEM photos of ZnIn2S4, showing that the ZnIn2S4 catalysts are smooth nanowires with diameters about 50 nm. Fig. 2C and D provide the SEM/TEM photos of ZnIn2S4/GQD5, showing that the catalysts are nanowires with diameters about 50 nm containing lots of nanoparticles on the surface. The nanoparticles on the surface of ZnIn2S4 nanowires are the incorporated GQDs, which is confirmed by the elemental mapping images shown in Fig. 2E-2F. These images demonstrate that the elements of Zn, In, S and C distribute uniformly in the ZnIn2S4/GQD5 catalyst. The similar diameters of ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD5 indicate that the incorporated GQDs barely influence the growth of ZnIn2S4 nanowires although it enhances the crystallization of ZnIn2S4.

The surface composition and chemical states of the prepared catalysts were represented by XPS measurements using ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD5 as representative catalysts. Fig. S1 gives the full-scale XPS results of the prepared ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD5 catalysts. The presence of S 2p, In 3d, Zn 2p and C 1s is confirmed by the spectra. Fig. 3A, B, C and D provide the specific XPS spectra of Zn 2p, In 2d, S 2p and C 1s for the prepared ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD5 catalysts. For ZnIn2S4, the peaks locating at 1022.2/1045.3 eV relate with Zn 2p1/2/2p3/2. The difference of binding energies is about 23.1 eV, suggesting a typical Zn2+ in ZnIn2S4 [41]. Comparing with ZnIn2S4, the binding energy peaks of Zn 2p1/2/2p3/2 for ZnIn2S4/GQD5 shift to 1022.5/1045.6 eV, respectively. For ZnIn2S4, the peaks locating at 444.6/452.1 eV relate with In 3d3/2/3d5/2 [2]. Comparing with ZnIn2S4, the peaks of In 3d3/2/3d5/2 for ZnIn2S4/GQD5 shift to 444.8/452.4 eV, respectively. The S 2p1/2/2p3/2 respectively locate at 161.6/163.0 eV for ZnIn2S4 [31] and they respectively shift to 161.8/163.2 eV for ZnIn2S4/GQD5. Fig. 3D provides the specific XPS spectra of C 1s for GQDs and ZnIn2S4/GQD5. The peak at 284.6 eV relates with the compensated carbon from the XPS instrument. For GQDs, the other two peaks relate with the C-OH and Sp2-hybridized carbon in CO, respectively [42]. For ZnIn2S4/GQD5, the C-OH binding energy peak nearly disappears and the CO binding energy beak weakens, meaning that the stable bonds have been formed between GQDs and ZnIn2S4 [36]. It is known that the binding energy is induced by Coulomb attraction between the nucleus and outer electron of atom, and thus the different binding energies mean different electron densities [43]. Based on this, the direction of movements for the charge carriers could be speculated by the changes of binding energies. Comparing with ZnIn2S4, the binding energy peaks of Zn 2p, In 2d and S 2p for ZnIn2S4/GQD5 shift to higher values, indicating the interfacial electron transfers from ZnIn2S4 to GQDs [44]. These electron transfers generally help to separate photoinduced
e −
−
h +
pairs and consequently bring about higher catalytic performance than that of pure ZnIn2S4.

The incorporated GQDs also influence light absorbance and band gap of the prepared catalysts, which are represented by UV–vis absorbance spectra. Fig. 4A provides absorbance spectra of the prepared GQDs, ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs. The prepared GQDs show excellent absorbance ability in UV–vis light region. The prepared ZnIn2S4 nanowires also show visible light absorbance and has an absorbance edge about 546 nm. Following the increasing GQDs contents, the absorbance edge of the composited ZnIn2S4/GQDs nanowires moves to longer wavelength and the visible light absorbance ability enhances effectively. These suggest that more charge carriers may be generated by ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites than that of ZnIn2S4. In order to show their light absorbance abilities, the bandgaps (Eg) have been evaluated via Tauc equation [34]:
(1)
αhν
2
= A
hν −
E g

Fig. 4B provides Tauc plots of the prepared catalysts. The details for the evaluation using Tauc equation are provided in Supporting Materials. The Eg values for the ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs nanowires are provided in Fig. 4B and Table S1. On the basis of XPS results, the bonds between GQDs and ZnIn2S4 are formed, which leads to the decrease of Eg on account of the additional energy states below the conduction band and above the valence band [5]. In comparison with ZnIn2S4, the narrowing Eg of ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites indicates more efficient light absorbance and thus higher photocatalytic activity.

The generation of charge carriers upon light irradiation is generally evaluated by the photocurrent response. Fig. 5A provides the photocurrent densities of the prepared catalysts over three cycles under simulate sunlight irradiation. For GQDs, no photocurrent upon light excitation, indicating that the fabricated GQDs don't show obvious photochemical effect. For ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs, the stable photocurrent is generated instantly as soon as light irradiation is provided, indicating that they are sensitive to light irradiation. Once the light is turned on, lots of charge carriers from the catalysts are generated. They accumulate transiently, which results in the formation of photocurrent spikes [6]. The photo response is reversible in the light on-off cycles, indicating that the prepared catalysts have good photo-stability. The photocurrent density for the ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites is higher than that of ZnIn2S4. One reason for this is the incorporated GQDs bring about more efficient light absorbance, as discussed in the optical properties. This means more generation of photoinduced
e −
−
h +
pairs and thus induces higher photocurrent density. The other reason is that the incorporated GQDs act as electron acceptors, and thus the photoinduced e− transfers to GQDs in the ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites. This process suppresses uncontrolled recombination of
e −
−
h +
pairs and thus also leads to the enhancement of photocurrent density. Subsequently, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were measured. The semicircle radii of the ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites are smaller than that of ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 5B), indicating the faster electron transfers in the ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites. This means rapid charge diffusion to the active sites on account of the incorporation of highly conductive GQDs [23]. The ZnIn2S4/GQD5 shows lower photocurrent density and larger semicircle radius than that of ZnIn2S4/GQD4 although the ZnIn2S4/GQD5 has the higher efficient light absorbance and smaller Eg because that superfluous GQDs act as recombination centers for photoinduced carriers and the light absorbed by GQDs has a detrimental effect on the effective excitation of the ZnIn2S4 nanowires [10,45]. Among the prepared catalysts, the ZnIn2S4/GQD4 catalyst shows the highest photocurrent density and smallest semicircle radii of EIS spectrum, suggesting that it may have the highest photocatalytic activity.

The catalytic activities of catalysts in different catalysis processes were evaluated by degrading tetracycline hydrochloride. Fig. S2 provides absorbance spectra of the tetracycline hydrochloride solution after 30, 40, 50 and 60 minutes’ stirring. The intensities of the absorbance spectra barely change, indicating that the adsorption-desorption equilibrium has been obtained after 30 minutes’ stirring. Fig. 6 shows the dependence of tetracycline hydrochloride concentration on reaction time after adding different samples in sonocatalysis (A), photocatalysis (B) and sono-photocatalysis (C). The degradation efficiencies at different reaction times are provided in Table S2. Firstly, the tetracycline hydrochloride concentration decreases after a 30 minutes’ adsorption-desorption equilibrium in catalysis processes after adding prepared samples, which results from the adsorption of nanostructured particles. The GQDs shows the highest adsorption and the adsorption abilities of the ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites increase following the increases of GQDs content. This order is induced by the different specific surface areas. Fig. S3 provides the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples. The specific surface areas of GQDs, ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs composites are calculated as 96.36, 21.23, 27.35, 32.12, 36.81, 41.37 and 45.04 m2/g, respectively. The larger specific surface area means the better adsorption ability, which generally leads to more reaction sites. Secondly, the addition of catalysts leads to higher degradation efficiency and the sonocatalysis efficiency with the presence of ZnIn2S4/GQD4 is highest. Thirdly, the GODs don't show photocatalytic activity but the incorporated GQDs on the surface of ZnIn2S4 nanowires bring about higher activity in photocatalysis and sono-photocatalysis. And the ZnIn2S4/GQD4 catalyst shows the highest catalytic activity in photocatalysis and sono-photocatalysis. Fourthly, the degradation efficiencies are enhanced highly in sono-photocatalysis comparing with sonocatalysis and photocatalysis. The stability was then investigated by cyclic experiments. Fig. S4 provides the XRD pattern and TEM image of the ZnIn2S4/GQD4 after four cycles, and its degradation efficiencies in four recycles. The results indicate that the phase, microstructure and degradation efficiency barely change. These results demonstrate that the prepared photocatalysts have excellent stability. The comparisons of tetracycline hydrochloride degradation by ZnIn2S4/GQD4 with some reported catalysts is provided in Table S3. The results indicate that the prepared catalysts own excellent activity to degrade tetracycline hydrochloride.

The catalytic activities of different catalysts can be revealed directly by the reaction constant (k). Fig. 7 provides the curves of Ln
C 0
/
C t
vs. time for different catalysts. The details of calculation for k are shown by Supporting Materials. And the k values for different catalysts in different catalysis processes are shown by Table S2. The ZnIn2S4/GQD4 shows the highest k values in sonocatalysis, photocatalysis and sono-photocatalysis. The k values of ZnIn2S4/GQD4 in sonocatalysis (0.02129 min−1), photocatalysis (0.01838 min−1) and sono-photocatalysis (0.08508 min−1) are about 1.27, 1.59 and 2.04 times as same as those of ZnIn2S4 (0.01675, 0.01154 and 0.04177 min−1, respectively), indicating that the incorporated GQDs enhance the degradation efficiency on account of enhancive photochemical effect. The k value of ZnIn2S4/GQD4 in sono-photocatalysis is about 3.99 and 4.63 times as same as that in sonocatalysis and photocatalysis, indicating that the synergistic sonochemical and photochemical effects enhance the degradation efficiency effectively. For evaluating this fact, the synergy index can be calculated via the following formula:
(2)
Synergy index =
K
Sono + Photo
/
K Sono
+
K Photo
where KSono+Photo, KSono and KPhoto represent k values of catalyst in sono-photocatalysis, sonocatalysis and photocatalysis [46]. The synergy index higher than one suggests the positive influence on degradation process. The synergy index for ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs are 1.48, 1.50, 1.65, 1.86, 2.14 and 2.11, respectively. These results indicate that hybrid catalysis system is positive to degradation comparing with individual system, which is conducted by the ultrasonic [47].

Trapping experiments were conducted for investigating reactive radicals in catalysis processes. Fig. S5 shows the trapping results of sonocatalysis (A), photocatalysis (B) and sono-photocatalysis (C) without catalysts and with the presence of GQDs, ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQD4. Clearly, the degradation efficiencies decrease highly in all catalysis processes after adding isopropanol (IPA, scavenger of •OH), indicating that •OH radical plays dominant role in degradation processes. After the addition of silver nitrate (AgNO3, scavenger of e−), ethylenediaminetetraacetate acid disodium (EDTA-2Na, scavenger of h+), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ, scavenger of •O2 −), and furfuryl alcohol (FFA, scavenger of 1O2), the degradation efficiencies in all catalysis processes barely change, indicating that radicals of e−, h+, •O2 −, and 1O2 don't play dominant roles in catalysis processes. Electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) measurements using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were carried out to further confirm the reactive radicals in catalysis processes. Fig. 8 provides the ESR spectra of DMPO-•OH in different catalysis processes. The relative intensities are different in the same catalysis process with the presence of different catalysts and in different catalysis processes with the presence of the same catalyst. The discussion about it will be provided in the following part.

From the above experimental results, the possible mechanism and reasons for the different catalytic activities were speculated. Generally, acoustic cavitation occurs if ultrasonic passes a liquid medium, which induces the production of cavitation bubbles in an ultrasonic field. They grow fleetly and collapse instantaneously, which induces high temperature and high pressure. This leads to the pyrolysis of tetracycline hydrochloride and water, and thus results in the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride and production of reactive radicals (i.e. •OH, H• and HO2 •) [48]. The produced reactive radicals further degrade tetracycline hydrochloride via hysroxylation and oxidation processes. The trapping experiment results indicate that •OH plays the dominant role in sonocatalysis process without the presence of catalysts (generally named sonolysis). The following reactions may occur for the production of •OH.
(3)
H 2
O
→ Ultrasonic
• OH +
H •
(4)
H •
+
O 2
→ H
O 2 •
(5)
H
O 2 •
+ H
O 2 •
→
H 2
O 2
+
O 2
(6)
H 2
O 2
→ Heat
2 • OH

With the presence of catalyst, the nuclei are produced preferentially at the solid surface or phase boundaries, which leads to the production of a larger amount of cavitation bubbles and reactive radical of •OH [46]. As shown in Fig. 8A, the signal intensities are higher with the presence of catalysts than that of blank, indicating that the addition of catalyst benefits to the production of reactive radical. However, the influence of catalyst on the production of reactive radical is different, which should relate with the different photochemical effects. Sonoluminescence with emission wavelength in ultraviolet to near-infrared region is generated synchronously when bubbles collapse instantaneously [13]. The light coming from the sonoluminescence excites catalysts to produce
e −
−
h +
pairs. As shown in Fig. 5, GQDs don't show photo response to light. This means that the sonoluminescence doesn't leads to the generation of
e −
−
h +
pairs for GQDs. The enhancement of degradation in sonocatalysis after the addition of GQDs is induced by the enhanced sonoeffect and high adsorption ability. For ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs, upon excitation of light coming from sonoluminescence, electrons move from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of ZnIn2S4 and holes are leaved in VB:
(7)
Catalyst
→ hλ
e −
+
h +

And the following reactions occur if the conduction band potential (ECB) is less negative than that of
O 2
/ •
O 2 −
(−0.33 eV vs. NHE) and the valence band potential (EVB) is more positive than that of H2O/•OH (2.38 eV vs. NHE) [49].
(8)
e −
+
O 2
→ •
O 2 −
(9)
h +
+
H 2
O → • OH

The details of the calculation for ECB and EVB are provided in Supporting Materials and the corresponding ECB and EVB for ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs are provided in Table S1. From the results, the band structures of ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs are illustrated in Fig. 9 , indicating that reaction (8) occurs but reaction (9) is forbidden. However, the trapping experiment results indicate that the h+ barely influence degradation efficiency and •OH plays dominant role in degradation process. Thus, the following reactions occur, which transform h+ and •
O 2 −
to •OH.
(10)
2 •
O 2 −
+ 2
H 2
O →
H 2
O 2
+ 2 O
H −
+
O 2
(11)
h +
+ O
H −
→ • OH

Meanwhile, the produced H2O2 was transformed into •OH via reaction (6). On account of photoinduced
e −
−
h +
pairs, more •OH reactive radicals are produced in catalysis processes with the presence of ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/GQDs, which results in higher ESR intensities and more efficient degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride. It should be noted that the ZnIn2S4/GQDs show higher activity in sonocatalysis, which is induced by the higher efficient separation of photoinduced charge carriers. As discussed in XPS results, stable bonds have been formed between GQDs/ZnIn2S4 and thus the interfacial electron transfers from ZnIn2S4 to GQDs occur, which suppress the rapid recombination of
e −
−
h +
pairs. This has been confirmed by the photocurrent responses and EIS spectra, as shown in Fig. 5. High efficient separation of charge carriers leads to the more efficient production of reactive radicals. As shown in Fig. 8A, the intensity of ESR curve for ZnIn2S4/GQD4 is higher than that of ZnIn2S4, suggesting that more •OH reactive radicals are produced.

In photocatalysis, no ESR signal of DMPO-•OH is detected without catalyst and with the presence of GQDs, demonstrating that catalyst is essential and GQDs don't show catalytic activity in photocatalysis. In photocatalysis, the intensity of ESR curve for ZnIn2S4/GQD4 is also higher than that of ZnIn2S4. The reason is same as that of sonocatalysis, more efficient separation of charge carriers results in production of a larger amount of •OH reactive radical. But the ZnIn2S4/GQD5 catalyst shows lower photocatalytic activity than that of ZnIn2S4/GQD4 catalyst, which is induced perhaps by the self-aggregation of GQDs on the surface of ZnIn2S4. And the reason has been discussed in photocurrent and EIS analysis. The ZnIn2S4/GQD4 shows highest photocurrent density and smallest semicircle radius of EIS spectrum, indicating that it has highest separation efficiency of charge carriers and fastest electron transfers. In sono-photocatalysis, the intensities of ESR curves are higher than those of in sonocatalysis and photocatalysis, indicating that synergistic sonochemical and photochemical effects result in higher degradation efficiency than that of single process.

The degradation pathways were also speculated on the basis of experimental results using high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS). From MS spectra, fifteen intermediates are detected in catalysis process and three possible pathways are proposed. Fig. S6 provides the MS spectra of intermediates with different m/z values. Fig. 10 provides molecular structures of the intermediates and the proposed pathways. In a catalysis process, the tetracycline was generated firstly by removing HCl in tetracycline hydrochloride. Then, three possible degradation processes were formed by breaking different bonds and groups, which includes dual CC bond on the benzene ring, hydroxyl groups at the side of methyl group in cyclohexanone and dimethylamino groups (form amino). Subsequently, intermediates with minor m/z values were generated by deamination, dealkylation, dehydration, and ring-opening processes on account of attacks of reactive species [50]. Finally, intermediates with smaller molecular were formed and mineralized to CO2 and H2O with the presence of catalyst.

Composite ZnIn2S4/GQDs nanowires were synthesized using ultrasonic-assistant hydrothermal route. The XRD, Raman, TEM and XPS results indicate the successful synthesis of ZnIn2S4 nanowires and successful incorporation of GQDs on the surface of ZnIn2S4 nanowires. The BET analysis indicates that the incorporated GQDs bring about increase of the specific surface area, which results in more reactive sites. Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorbance spectra, photocurrent response and EIS demonstrate that the incorporated GQDs not only benefit to the enhancement of light absorbance, but also promote separation of photoinduced charge carriers. As a result, the ZnIn2S4/GQDs nanowires show higher catalytic activity than that of pure ZnIn2S4 nanowires. The synergistic sonochemical and photochemical effects can enhance the degradation efficiency effectively. In catalysis processes, reactive radical of •OH plays dominant roles. Three possible degradation ways were speculated, includes dual CC bond on the benzene ring, hydroxyl groups at the side of methyl group in cyclohexanone and dimethylamino groups.

Yujie Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Lingling Sun: Project administration, Data curation. Xiaoli Li: Software, Project administration. Xianke Sun: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (22B140009, 24B140018), and the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (242300420674).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jwpe.2024.105807.

Chin. Chem. Lett.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile fabrication of Bi nanoparticle-decorated g-C3N4 photocatalysts for effective tetracycline hydrochloride degradation: environmental factors, degradation mechanism, pathways and biotoxicity evaluation
